---
title: "FW 536 — Day 2 morning lab (v2 · forest & songbird) — Your Name"
author: "Your Name Here"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Day 2 morning lab — Continuous distributions problem set

**Problem set version:** **v2 — forest & songbird theme**.
(The companion version is [v3 — aquatic & fisheries](problem_set_v3.html); you choose whichever theme fits your interests.)

This template matches [`problem_set_v2.html`](problem_set_v2.html). Read each problem statement there, write your work below, **Knit** to HTML, and upload the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.

For setup help see the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html).

---

# Setup

```{r}
# Load packages you'll need. Add or remove as appropriate.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(MASS)        # for glm.nb
library(effects)
library(emmeans)
```

---

# Problem 1 — Tree DBH on a 1-hectare plot

> You map every stem > 5 cm DBH on a hectare of mixed Douglas-fir / western hemlock forest. The empirical DBH distribution is strongly right-skewed: many small understory stems, a few enormous overstory trees, with a long right tail.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 2 — Aboveground biomass vs basal area

> For 60 forested plots you have aboveground biomass (Mg/ha) and basal area (m²/ha). Biomass spans 5 to 800 Mg/ha. Two analysts fit:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 3 — Black-throated gray warbler counts on point counts

> From 120 ten-minute point counts in oak woodland, you fit

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 4 — Nest success of song sparrow vs nest height

> 250 monitored song-sparrow nests; outcome is fledged (1) or failed (0). Predictor: nest height in cm. Logistic regression returns intercept $-0.50$ and slope $+0.025$ per cm.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 5 — Acorn mast counts from individual oaks

> You collect acorn counts from 80 individual white oaks over one fall. Mean = 350, variance = 18,000. Both fits use a single intercept (no predictors).

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# (Optional) Embed a photo of hand-written work

For problems easier to do on paper, photograph and embed:

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE, out.width="60%", fig.cap="Hand-derivation for Problem N"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/problemN_derivation.jpg")
```

(Change `eval=FALSE` to `eval=TRUE` after saving an image. See the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html) for details.)

---

# Submission checklist

- [ ] Answered every morning problem from `problem_set_v2.html`.
- [ ] Knitted to HTML without errors.
- [ ] Photos (if any) embedded with `self_contained: true` in the YAML.
- [ ] Uploaded the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.
